title: Your First Map Investigation: Reading Your World
description: Use a map to investigate geographic patterns in your local area and the wider world. This session builds map literacy, geographic thinking, and climate knowledge.

steps.instruction: Open a world map (digital or printed). Locate the equator, prime meridian, and your location. Read about latitude and longitude: what do these lines measure and why do they form a global grid?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can read coordinates (e.g., 51°N, 0°E = London) and explain that latitude measures distance from equator and longitude from prime meridian
steps.hint: Latitude runs east-west (horizontal lines); longitude runs north-south (vertical lines). Together they identify any point on Earth

steps.instruction: Notice how the map represents a sphere on a flat surface. Compare two world maps with different projections. Read about map types and projections: why does every flat map distort something?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can explain that projections must sacrifice accuracy in area, shape, distance, or direction — there is no perfect flat map of a sphere
steps.hint: Greenland appears huge on Mercator projections but is smaller than Africa on equal-area projections

steps.instruction: Look at a population density map. Identify the most and least densely populated regions. Read about population and settlement: what physical geography factors explain the patterns you see?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can correlate high population density with temperate climates, arable land, and access to water, and explain why deserts and frozen regions are sparsely settled
steps.hint: Most of Earth's population lives in a relatively small band of temperate, coastal, and river valley areas

steps.instruction: Find a climate zone map. Identify your climate zone and two others in different parts of the world. Read about climate zones: what determines which climate zone a region belongs to?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can name your climate zone and explain that latitude, altitude, and ocean proximity are the primary determinants
steps.hint: Tropical zones near the equator receive direct sunlight year-round; polar zones receive sunlight at low angles, making them cold

steps.instruction: Find the major tectonic plate boundaries on a map. Overlay with a map of volcanoes and earthquake zones. Read about plate tectonics: why do these phenomena cluster at plate boundaries?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can explain that earthquakes and volcanoes concentrate at plate boundaries where plates collide, separate, or slide past each other
steps.hint: The 'Ring of Fire' around the Pacific Ocean is a convergent plate boundary zone — where plates collide and one subducts under the other
